agent1_snippets.title: Amniocentesis -- Technique or Treatment
agent1_snippets.content: Amniocentesis in twin pregnancies requires the determination of chorionicity and amnionicity. In dichorionic twins, there are two methods: single-needle technique and two-needle insertion technique. In the single needle technique, after collecting amniotic fluid from one sac, the syringe with the amniotic fluid of the first fetus is removed, and the needle pierces through the septum separating the two amniotic sacs to collect the amniotic fluid from the amniotic sac of the second fetus. This technique has a risk of contamination from the first sac. In the two-needle insertion technique, after collecting amniotic fluid from one sac, a small amount of blue-colored indigo carmine dye is injected into the sac of the first twin. Then the needle has pierced the skin and is placed into the sac of the second twin, and fluid is withdrawn. Repeat sampling of blue amniotic fluid allows the operator to know if the amniotic fluid of the first fetus is sampled twice. [30] [31]

agent1_snippets.title: Sonography Evaluation of Amniotic Fluid -- Introduction
agent1_snippets.content: In the early stages of pregnancy, the composition of amniotic fluid resembles a complex dialysate derived from maternal serum. As the fetus grows, changes occur in the fluid's composition; notably, sodium concentration and osmolality decrease while urea, creatinine, and uric acid levels increase. [4] [5] Amniotic fluid also contains various steroid and protein hormones. [6] [7] Initially, it has little to no particulate matter, but by 16 weeks gestation, a significant number of cells shed from the amnion, skin, and tracheobronchial tree are present. These cells are crucial for antenatal diagnosis and serve as a source of DNA for karyotype analysis following amniocentesis. [8] Typically, fetuses do not defecate during pregnancy; however, if under severe stress, they may pass meconium. This material contains bile pigments that can stain the amniotic fluid green, indicating fetal stress. [9] The regulation of amniotic fluid volume (AFV) involves 3 primary mechanisms: placental control of water and solute transfer, fetal contributions through urine production and swallowing, and maternal factors affecting fluid balance.

agent1_snippets.title: Sonography Evaluation of Amniotic Fluid -- Introduction
agent1_snippets.content: Amniotic fluid is critical during pregnancy to provide a protective and nourishing environment for the developing fetus. It maintains a sterile space, regulates temperature, and cushions against external shocks. Amniotic fluid facilitates fetal movement, essential for musculoskeletal development, and supports the growth of organs, primarily the lungs. [1] [2] [1] The amount of amniotic fluid gradually increases until approximately 34 weeks gestation, after which it slightly decreases until 40 weeks and then declines more sharply after 42 weeks. [3]

agent1_snippets.title: Amniotic Fluid Index(Archived) -- Introduction
agent1_snippets.content: Throughout intrauterine development, the fetus exists in a compartment filled with amniotic fluid, allowing free movement. [1] This fluid acts as a protective cushion against potential injuries, helps maintain a stable temperature, and is inhaled and swallowed by the fetus, contributing to normal lung development. [2] Amniotic fluid is a dynamic substance, with its volume and chemical makeup regulated within narrow limits. [3] The amount of amniotic fluid gradually increases until approximately 34 weeks of gestation, after which it slightly decreases until the 40th week and then declines more sharply until the 42nd week. [4]

agent1_snippets.title: Amniocentesis -- Introduction -- Amniocentesis
agent1_snippets.content: The amniotic fluid obtained consists of fetal exfoliated cells, transudates, fetal urine, and lung secretions. Amniocentesis can be performed from 15 weeks of gestation to delivery, with an attributable risk of loss in experienced hands of 0.13% in singletons. Earlier the attributable risk is greater than chorion villus sampling (CVS), which provides similar data from 11 to 15 weeks. There is an increased risk of amnionic fluid leakage of 1 to 2%, most of which corresponds to decreased activity in days and fetal demise, which is usually much rarer than the risk of demise attributable to the indication for the procedure, except in low-risk women with no maternal risks indicating the procedure, and talipes equinovarus, presumably from oligohydramnios. [5]

agent1_snippets.title: Amniocentesis -- Introduction -- Chorionic Villus Sampling
agent1_snippets.content: It is a prenatal invasive procedure and is done under ultrasound guidance. This procedure uses ultrasonography to guide the catheter or needle into the chorion frondosum. It is done abdominally and is followed by tissue aspiration (chorionic villi) for genetic or chromosomal analysis with a syringe containing tissue culture media. It is done in the first trimester for prenatal diagnosis between 10 to 14 weeks. Depending on the position of the uterus and bladder, the patient's gestational age, and placental localization, it can be performed transabdominally or transcervically.

agent1_snippets.title: Amniocentesis -- Clinical Significance
agent1_snippets.content: Amniocentesis procedure is relatively safe, with fewer complications among experienced hands. The location of the placenta is an important factor in amniocentesis. While performing the procedure, one should try to avoid penetration of the placenta. The anterior and fundal placenta is associated with more complications, including multiple pricks and blood-stained liquor; however, it is not associated with increased fetal loss rates. [37] Passing the needle through the placenta is slightly associated with increased preterm birth rates. [38]

agent1_snippets.title: Amniocentesis -- Indications -- Therapeutic Indications
agent1_snippets.content: In hydramnios, it has a therapeutic role in relieving maternal discomfort and the instillation of intraamniotic drugs. Decompression amniocentesis in twin oligohydramnios-polyhydramnios sequence (TOPS): as it decreases the volume of amniotic fluid in the polyhydramnios sac, amniotic fluid pressures are decreased in both the sacs resulting in increased placental thickness thus improving uteroplacental circulation and improving fetal outcome. [19] It can help in amnioinfusion in oligohydramnios to prevent fetal lung hypoplasia and umbilical cord compression during labor. It is indicated for fetal blood transfusion in fetuses having severe hemolysis.

agent1_snippets.title: Amniotic Fluid Index(Archived) -- Specimen Requirements and Procedure -- Alternative Measurements of Amniotic Fluid Volume
agent1_snippets.content: A single deepest pocket is used in pregnancies less than 24 weeks or with multiple gestations. [28] The technique used to measure a single deepest pocket, also called a maximum vertical pocket, is identical to the measurement of amniotic fluid amounts in the 4 quadrants used to determine an amniotic fluid index. The entire uterus should be examined, and the single deepest vertical pocket of fluid should be identified and measured. A normal single deepest pocket is 2 to 8 cm. A measurement less than 2 cm indicates oligohydramnios, whereas a measurement greater than 8 cm is polyhydramnios. [29] [30]

agent1_snippets.content: Additional recycling pathways include the intramembranous pathway of the placenta, umbilical cord, and fetal skin between the amniotic fluid and fetal blood (400 mL per day). [15] Amniotic fluid increases at a linear rate initially in the first half of pregnancy and continues to increase until the 36th or 38th week of pregnancy with an average maximal volume of 800 mL. [10] [15] Next, amniotic fluid can decrease by 8% per week, down to as low as 400 mL in gestation, near week 42. [10]

agent1_snippets.title: Amniocentesis -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Physiology of Amniotic Fluid
agent1_snippets.content: Maternal and fetal compartments are essential in forming amniotic fluid in the first trimester of pregnancy. Fetal skin is non-keratinized in earlier weeks, allowing free transfer of water and other small molecules and solutes through the amnion and chorionic. [8] Amniotic fluid is similar to maternal and fetal extracellular fluid and functions as a nonsterile aqueous electrolyte solution. During the second trimester of pregnancy, the diffusion process ceases as keratinization of fetal skin occurs, making the fetal skin impervious to water and other solutes.

agent1_snippets.title: Amniotic Fluid Embolism -- Treatment / Management
agent1_snippets.content: Prompt and effective cardiopulmonary resuscitation is the cornerstone of managing an acute AFE. Chest compressions should be initiated immediately, providing 100 to 120 compressions per minute with 1 ventilation every 6 seconds. It is crucial to avoid excessive ventilation, as this can lead to a decrease in cardiac output. [20]

agent1_snippets.title: Amniotomy -- Introduction
agent1_snippets.content: Amniotomy, also known as artificial rupture of membranes (AROM) and by the lay description "breaking the water," is the intentional rupture of the amniotic sac by an obstetrical provider. This procedure is common during labor management and has been performed by obstetrical providers for at least a few hundred years. The reasons for the intentional rupture of the amniotic sac during labor are multifold and include, but are not limited to, influencing the speed of labor, allowing for more direct monitoring of fetal status, and qualitative assessment of the amniotic fluid.

agent1_snippets.title: Amniotomy -- Anatomy and Physiology
agent1_snippets.content: The amniotic cavity is an enclosed space within the uterus in which the fetus develops and is protected during the antepartum period. The amniotic cavity is comprised of a dual-layer membrane, which includes an inner layer known as the amnion and an outer layer known as the chorion. This potential space forms early in pregnancy and is filled with serous fluid during the first few weeks of pregnancy. With further development of the fetus, specifically the fetal urinary system, the fluid in this potential space increases as the developing fetus excretes urine. Fetal urine comprises most of the amniotic fluid. Typically, this barrier remains intact throughout the duration of gestation, and the amniotic membranes will spontaneously rupture, releasing the amniotic fluid either immediately preceding spontaneous labor or sometimes after the onset of spontaneous labor. [1] [2] [3]

agent1_snippets.title: Amniocentesis -- Contraindications
agent1_snippets.content: There are no absolute contraindications for amniocentesis. Relative contraindications for amniocentesis are: Hepatitis B and HIV infections can be transmitted from maternal circulation to the fetus during the procedure. It should be deferred in HIV-infected patients until antiretroviral treatment is started and the viral load substantially decreases. Highly active antiretroviral therapy for HIV greatly reduces maternal to fetal transmission rates. [20] [21] [22] Decreased amniotic fluid (oligohydramnios) [23] Oral anticoagulation therapy must be stopped 48 to 72 hours before the procedure, and patients may be shifted to low molecular weight heparin.

agent1_snippets.title: Amniocentesis -- Continuing Education Activity
agent1_snippets.content: Amniocentesis is the aspiration of amniotic fluid from the amniotic cavity and is the most common invasive fetal testing procedure. Amniocentesis is an invasive technique. It is usually performed to diagnose or exclude fetal aneuploidy or other fetal genetic, biochemical or infectious risks. Prenatal diagnosis enables the diagnosis of a broad spectrum of chromosomal abnormalities, gene disorders, X-linked conditions, neural tube defects, and infections to be made before the birth of the fetus. The indications grow with progress in these fields. This activity outlines the procedure, reviews its complication rates, describes the complexity of obtaining truly informed consent, and examines the role of the healthcare team in improving care for high-risk patients who undergo this invasive procedure.

agent1_snippets.title: Polyhydramnios -- Treatment / Management -- Amnioreduction
agent1_snippets.content: For symptomatic patients with severe polyhydramnios, amnioreduction can be considered. [2] Amnioreduction is an ultrasound-guided procedure that removes amniotic fluid through a large transabdominal needle, typically a 15-cm 20-gauge needle. [43] Typically, 1.5 and 3 L of fluid are removed during the procedure. However, amnioreduction does not address the underlying cause of excess amniotic fluid, so fluid frequently reaccumulates, limiting the procedure's efficacy. More than one procedure is required in 42% to 46% of individuals. [43] [44] Amnioreduction is generally not appropriate for asymptomatic patients or those with non-severe disease. [2]

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2347
agent2_snippets.content: Amnioinfusion, بزل السائل الأمنيوسي, and خزعة الزغابات المشيمية are all classified under the category of antepartum application, insertion or removal procedures. These interventions are performed before delivery to modify, sample, or assess the amniotic environment. Amnioinfusion involves the introduction of fluid into the amniotic cavity, بزل السائل الأمنيوسي (amniocentesis) entails aspiration of amniotic fluid for diagnostic testing, and خزعة الزغابات المشيمية (chorionic villus sampling) involves obtaining placental tissue for genetic analysis. Recognizing that these procedures share a common therapeutic and diagnostic purpose helps clinicians select appropriate antepartum interventions and coordinate patient care.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2420
agent2_snippets.content: Amnioinfusion and خزعة الزغابات المشيمية (chorionic villus sampling) are both categorized as Antepartum application, insertion or removal procedures. These procedures are performed before delivery and involve the insertion of instruments or substances into the maternal‑fetal environment for therapeutic or diagnostic purposes. Amnioinfusion specifically refers to the infusion of fluid into the amniotic cavity to alleviate conditions such as oligohydramnios or improve fetal monitoring, while خزعة الزغابات المشيمية involves the sampling of placental tissue to obtain fetal genetic material for early prenatal diagnosis. Both interventions are considered child procedures within the broader class of antepartum application, insertion or removal procedures, highlighting their role in prenatal care and fetal assessment.

agent2_snippets.content: Amniotomy at delivery is classified as an obstetrics incision, representing a specific procedural approach used to artificially rupture the membranes during labor. The broader category of obstetrics incision encompasses additional child procedures, including البزل القلبي (cardiac puncture) and بزل السائل الأمنيوسي (amniotic fluid tap). Together, these interventions illustrate the range of techniques that fall under obstetric incisions, highlighting their roles in managing both maternal and fetal conditions during pregnancy and delivery.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 2275
agent2_snippets.content: Perinatal Terminology encompasses a range of specific obstetric conditions and descriptors. The term انْصِمامٌ بالسَّائِلِ السَّلَوِيّ refers to amniotic fluid embolism, a rare but life‑threatening complication of labor. حمل بطني denotes an abdominal pregnancy, a form of ectopic gestation in which the fetus develops within the abdominal cavity. حمل مطول describes a prolonged pregnancy, typically defined as gestation beyond 42 weeks, which carries increased risks for both mother and fetus. رحى عدارية خبيثة identifies a malignant uterine fibroid (leiomyosarcoma) that can affect pregnancy outcomes. قلة السائل الأمنيوسي indicates oligohydramnios, a condition of reduced amniotic fluid volume that may signal fetal compromise. مشيمة ملتصقة refers to placenta accreta, a disorder in which the placenta abnormally adheres to the uterine wall, leading to potential hemorrhage at delivery. Recognizing these concepts as part of Perinatal Terminology aids clinicians in diagnosing, monitoring, and managing high‑risk pregnancies.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 4 -- Community 2788
agent2_snippets.content: Amniotomy at delivery is an artificial rupture of the membranes (تمزق الأغشية الاصطناعي), a deliberate obstetric procedure in which a clinician intentionally breaks the amniotic sac to accelerate labor. By creating a controlled opening, amniotomy facilitates the descent of the fetus, enhances the effectiveness of uterine contractions, and allows for more accurate fetal monitoring. The technique is typically employed when labor is progressing slowly, when there is a need to augment contractions, or to expedite delivery for maternal or fetal indications. While amniotomy can shorten the duration of labor and reduce the need for pharmacologic induction, it also carries potential risks such as infection, cord prolapse, or excessive fetal head compression, and therefore is performed after careful assessment of the clinical situation and informed consent. Proper selection of candidates and adherence to aseptic technique are essential to maximize the benefits of this artificial membrane rupture in obstetric care.

Late effects of pregnancy, childbirth, and postpartum complications (التأثير المتأخر لمضاعفات الحمل والولادة والنفاس) are themselves classified as pregnancy complications, underscoring the long‑term sequelae that can follow obstetric events. Amniotic cavity and membrane problems are classified as polyhydramnios and other problems of the amniotic cavity, which in turn are categorized as abnormal amniocentesis (البزل السّلوي غير الطبيعي). This classification highlights that excessive amniotic fluid and related membrane abnormalities are often identified through abnormal amniotic fluid sampling. Polyhydramnios and other problems of the amniotic cavity also have child conditions such as premature rupture of membranes (تمزق الأغشية الباكر) and infection in the amniotic cavity (عدوى في الجوف السلوي), indicating a hierarchical relationship where fluid overload can lead to membrane rupture and subsequent infection.

Within this framework, تمزق الأغشية الاصطناعي (artificial membrane rupture) is identified as a particular type of Procedure by site, indicating that it is organized according to the site of intervention. The amnion is specified as the direct procedure site of تمزق الأغشية الاصطناعي, meaning that the artificial membrane rupture is carried out directly on the amniotic membrane. This hierarchy underscores that artificial membrane rupture is an organ‑specific, site‑based procedure targeting the amnion, and it illustrates how procedural classifications are linked to the anatomical structures on which they are performed.

agent2_snippets.content: Funic presentation, also referred to as cord presentation, is classified as a type of سوء المجيء الجنيني (abnormal fetal presentation). It occurs when the umbilical cord descends into the birth canal ahead of the fetus, increasing the risk of cord compression, fetal distress, and delivery complications. Recognizing funic presentation as a subset of سوء المجيء الجنيني is important for obstetric management, prompting careful monitoring and, when necessary, interventions such as cesarean delivery to protect maternal and neonatal health.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 1050
agent2_snippets.content: Amnioinfusion is classified as an obstetric procedure and belongs to the Antepartum application, insertion or removal procedures, a subcategory of Antepartum Procedures that also includes amniocentesis, chorionic villus sampling, injection of amnion for termination of pregnancy (synonymous with saline‑solution abortion), and fetal reduction, the latter being a specific form of reduction of multiple pregnancy. Antepartum Procedures serve as a broader parent class for these interventions performed before labor. Additional obstetric procedures performed during the peripartum period include Cesarean hysterectomy, intrapartum fetal stimulation, manual fetal rotation, and cardiac puncture. Postpartum obstetric operation is a parent node encompassing interventions after delivery, such as removal of retained products of conception; this includes techniques like dilation of the cervix uteri combined with vacuum aspiration, which is a specific method for achieving product removal. The network links pre‑labor diagnostic and therapeutic actions, intra‑labor surgical and therapeutic actions, and post‑delivery management under the unified concept of obstetric procedures, facilitating clinician selection of appropriate techniques for diagnosis, pregnancy termination, fetal management, and immediate postpartum assessment and care.
